subject: Mathematics
contextNote: Sec 4 Math, 24 students. You modelled vector addition using both the tip-to-tail arrow method and component form — showing that a = (3, 4) and b = (1, −2) gives a + b = (4, 2), with magnitude |a + b| = √20. Students are now 24 minutes into a set of vector addition problems. The class has visibly different working styles — some draw arrow diagrams immediately, others work in component form, others describe direction and magnitude verbally.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: Component addition correct and resultant magnitude calculated. Has not drawn the arrow diagram. Done early.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: Arrow diagram drawn correctly with tip-to-tail method. Has not calculated the magnitude. Cannot connect the diagram to the component form.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Has written 'the resultant points roughly northeast' verbally but has no diagram, no components, and no magnitude.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Partial arrow diagram — has drawn the first vector correctly but the second vector starts from the origin rather than the tip of the first.

ep1.situation: 24 minutes into the lesson. You circulate and the working styles are strikingly different. Rajan has the component addition correct — (3, 4) + (1, −2) = (4, 2) — and has calculated the magnitude |a + b| = √20 = 2√5. He has not drawn the arrow diagram at all. Nur has drawn a clear tip-to-tail arrow diagram with the resultant correctly labelled, but she has not written the components or calculated the magnitude. She can visualise the addition but cannot translate it into numbers. Wei Jie has written 'the resultant points roughly northeast with a smaller y-component than the first vector' — a correct verbal description — but has no diagram, no components, and no magnitude anywhere on his page. Siti has drawn the first vector correctly but has started the second vector from the origin rather than from the tip of the first — a fundamental tip-to-tail error. Her diagram shows two separate vectors rather than a chain. A quick scan of the class: 7 students have complete component solutions with magnitudes like Rajan. 8 students have correct arrow diagrams but no components like Nur. 5 students have verbal descriptions but no representations like Wei Jie. 4 students have diagram errors like Siti. You have about 16 minutes of lesson time remaining.
ep1.evidence: The gap is not in understanding that vectors add — most students can describe or show the addition in some form. The gap is in representation: students are working in their preferred mode and not connecting across modes. The magnitude calculation requires integrating the arrow diagram with the component form, and most students cannot make that connection. Siti's tip-to-tail error suggests she has not understood the geometric meaning of vector addition.

choices.label: Two-step vector representation diagnostic
choices.elaboration: Stop the class and project a new problem: 'a = (3, 4), b = (1, −2). Find a + b and its magnitude.' Ask two questions in sequence: '(1) Calculate the resultant vector and its magnitude.' Then: '(2) How did you find it? Choose: (a) I added the components: (3+1, 4+(−2)) = (4, 2), then |a+b| = √(16+4) = √20. (b) I drew the tip-to-tail diagram and measured the resultant. (c) I described the direction and estimated the magnitude. (d) I am not sure.' Ask students to write both answers on paper. Use the pattern to see which representation each student defaults to and whether they can connect the representation to the magnitude calculation.
choices.tradeoff: The two-step structure reveals both the representation preference and the ability to connect the representation to the magnitude calculation. It gives you whole-class evidence of who can integrate across modes. But it halts the class and may overwhelm students like Siti who have a fundamental diagram error. The diagnostic also interrupts Rajan, who has already made the connection.

choices.label: Profile-matched vector tasks: arrow, component, verbal
choices.elaboration: Distribute differentiated task cards matched to each student's working style without stopping the class. Visual students like Nur receive an arrow-to-component bridge: 'Read the components from your diagram: the resultant goes ___ units right and ___ units up. Write this as a vector (x, y). Then calculate the magnitude using |v| = √(x² + y²).' Symbolic students like Rajan receive a component-to-arrow bridge: 'Plot your resultant vector (4, 2) as an arrow on a coordinate grid. Label the tip-to-tail construction. What does the diagram show about the direction of the resultant?' Verbal students like Wei Jie receive a verbal-to-representation bridge: 'You described the direction. Draw the tip-to-tail diagram that matches your description. Then write the components and calculate the magnitude.' Siti receives a tip-to-tail correction scaffold: 'Step 1: Draw vector a starting at the origin. Step 2: Draw vector b starting at the TIP of vector a (not the origin). Step 3: The resultant goes from the origin to the tip of b.'
choices.tradeoff: Profile-matched tasks give every student an entry point from their preferred representation and bridge toward the others. The tasks are respectful — they all require reasoning, not just copying. But the room will go quiet as students work individually, and you lose the opportunity to gather whole-class evidence of the representation gap. Siti's tip-to-tail error needs direct correction — the scaffold may help her draw the diagram correctly without understanding why the tip-to-tail rule works.

choices.label: Accountable Talk: connect arrow, component, and magnitude
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and assign triads — one student who prefers arrows, one who prefers components, one who prefers verbal reasoning. Ask each triad to explain the vector addition using the stem: 'The arrow diagram shows ___ because ___. The component form shows the same thing because ___. The magnitude is ___ because ___.' Each student must contribute from their preferred representation. Circulate and listen for triads where the connections are genuine versus triads where students are talking past each other. After 3 minutes, select one triad to share their chain with the class and run a brief IRF probe.
choices.tradeoff: Triad talk forces students to connect across representations through structured dialogue. The stem ensures each representation is addressed. But triad quality depends on the mix — if all three students are in the same representation mode, the connection will not be made. Siti, who has a fundamental diagram error, may echo her triad partners without correcting her tip-to-tail mistake.

A.consequence: The responses come in and the representation patterns are clear. 15 of 24 students calculated the resultant correctly. But only 8 of 24 could calculate the magnitude — and of those 8, 6 used components, 2 used the diagram. Nur drew the diagram correctly but chose option (b) — she cannot connect the diagram to the magnitude calculation. Rajan completed both parts correctly and chose option (a) with a clear explanation: 'I added the components to get (4, 2), then used Pythagoras: |a+b| = √(4² + 2²) = √20 = 2√5.' Wei Jie chose option (c) — he described the direction but could not calculate the magnitude. Siti chose option (d) — she does not know how to approach the problem. The evidence is now precise: the majority gap is in connecting the representation to the magnitude calculation, and Siti has a fundamental tip-to-tail error. 14 minutes remain.

choices.label: Whole-class reteach: arrow-component-magnitude chain
choices.elaboration: Address the representation-to-magnitude gap directly with the whole class. Draw all three representations on the board simultaneously: the tip-to-tail arrow diagram (showing the resultant as the closing vector), the component form (3+1=4, 4+(−2)=2, so resultant = (4,2)), and the magnitude calculation (|a+b| = √(4²+2²) = √20). Connect them explicitly: 'The arrow diagram shows the direction of the resultant. The components give you the exact values. The magnitude uses Pythagoras on the components.' Also correct the tip-to-tail rule: 'The second vector must start at the TIP of the first — not the origin.' Ask all students to re-attempt the problem using whichever representation they prefer, then write one sentence connecting their representation to the magnitude.
choices.tradeoff: Process-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) addresses the representation-to-magnitude gap directly and corrects Siti's tip-to-tail error simultaneously. It is efficient and will reach the 16 students who cannot calculate the magnitude. But it reteaches Rajan who already understands, and the simultaneous display of three representations may overwhelm Siti who is still fixing her diagram error.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) peer feedback: bridge representation to magnitude
choices.elaboration: Use the diagnostic evidence to form pairs: students who connected their representation to the magnitude (like Rajan) are paired with students who have a representation but no magnitude (like Nur). The stronger student gives AQS feedback: Affirm the representation ('Your arrow diagram is correct — the tip-to-tail construction and resultant are right'), Question the connection ('How do you calculate the magnitude from the diagram? What are the components of the resultant?'), Suggest the method ('Read the components from the diagram: the resultant goes 4 right and 2 up. Then use Pythagoras: √(4²+2²) = √20'). Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one pair to share their AQS exchange.
choices.tradeoff: AQS feedback activates the expertise in the room and gives Rajan a cognitively demanding task. The structure ensures feedback is specific and actionable. But Wei Jie, who has only a verbal description, has nothing for a peer to affirm in terms of a representation. Siti's tip-to-tail error needs direct correction — a peer may not notice the fundamental diagram error and may focus on the magnitude instead.

B.consequence: The profile-matched cards go out and the room shifts. Rajan receives the component-to-arrow bridge and plots the resultant (4, 2) on a coordinate grid. He draws the tip-to-tail construction and writes: 'The diagram shows the resultant pointing northeast — 4 units right and 2 units up. The angle is arctan(2/4) ≈ 27° from the horizontal.' Nur receives the arrow-to-component bridge and reads the components from her diagram: 4 right, 2 up. She writes (4, 2) and calculates |a+b| = √20. She looks pleased. Wei Jie receives the verbal-to-representation bridge and draws a rough tip-to-tail diagram. He writes the components (4, 2) and attempts the magnitude: √(4²+2²) = √20. But when you ask 'Why do we use Pythagoras for the magnitude?', he says 'Because the formula says so.' Siti receives the tip-to-tail correction scaffold and re-draws her diagram correctly — the second vector now starts at the tip of the first. But when you ask 'Why does the second vector start at the tip?', she points to the scaffold and says 'Because it says to.' The room is on-task but quiet. 15 minutes remain.

choices.label: IRF (Initiate-Response-Feedback) probe: connect arrow, component, and magnitude
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and run an IRF chain. Ask: 'Rajan, from your diagram — why does the magnitude use Pythagoras?' (Initiation). Then ask a second student: 'Can you connect that to the component form — what do the components represent geometrically?' (Response). Then ask a third student: 'And why does the second vector start at the tip of the first — what would happen if it started at the origin?' (Feedback). Build a class-constructed explanation linking all three representations to the magnitude calculation and the tip-to-tail rule. Write the connection on the board.
choices.tradeoff: The IRF chain leverages Rajan's diagram work and builds a shared explanation linking all three representations to the magnitude and the tip-to-tail rule. It gives the middle group a concrete, class-built connection. But the chain is driven by students who can articulate their reasoning — Siti, who just corrected her diagram using the scaffold, may follow the chain without understanding why the tip-to-tail rule works.

choices.label: Self-check: new vectors with all three representations
choices.elaboration: Give a new problem: 'a = (2, 3), b = (4, −1). Find a + b and its magnitude using at least two representations.' Ask students to write a self-check: 'I used ___ and ___ to answer this. I know my magnitude is correct because ___.' Collect the responses and scan them to see who can integrate across representations and who is still working in a single mode.
choices.tradeoff: The self-check application tests whether students can integrate across representations in a new problem. It will reveal Wei Jie's gap — he can draw the diagram but cannot explain why Pythagoras gives the magnitude. But Siti, who just corrected her diagram using the scaffold, will likely revert to the tip-to-tail error on the new problem without the scaffold to guide her.

C.consequence: The triads begin talking and the room gets louder. Rajan's triad works well — he explains the components, his partner explains the arrow diagram, and a third student connects both to the magnitude: 'The components give you the horizontal and vertical distances, and Pythagoras gives you the length of the resultant — which is the hypotenuse of the right triangle formed by the components.' Nur's triad has two visual students and one verbal student. They talk about the diagram but cannot connect it to the components or the magnitude — the verbal student says 'it points northeast' and the others agree. Wei Jie's triad has two verbal students and one symbolic student. Wei Jie says 'the resultant is northeast' and his triad partners nod, but no one draws a diagram or writes the components. Siti echoes her triad partners without correcting her diagram error. You circulate and hear the pattern: 6 triads have genuine cross-representation connections. 11 triads are working in a single mode. The talk has activated some connections but the depth varies. 14 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) feedback on single-mode reasoning
choices.elaboration: Write the most common single-mode explanation on the board anonymously: 'The resultant vector points northeast because the x-component is positive and the y-component is positive.' Ask the class to give AQS feedback: Affirm what is correct ('The direction description is right — positive x and y components mean northeast'), Question what is missing ('How do you calculate the exact magnitude? What is the length of the resultant?'), Suggest the connection ('Use the components to calculate the magnitude: |a+b| = √(x²+y²) — this is Pythagoras applied to the component right triangle'). Take responses from multiple students and build a complete AQS response. Then ask all students to add the magnitude calculation to their explanation.
choices.tradeoff: The AQS structure makes the missing representation-to-magnitude connection explicit and gives the single-mode triads a concrete model of what integrated reasoning looks like. It is based on real student reasoning, which makes it concrete. But it interrupts the triad work momentum. Siti's tip-to-tail error is not addressed by the AQS feedback — she will likely continue with the wrong diagram.

choices.label: Solo synthesis: vectors to arrow, component, and magnitude
choices.elaboration: Stop the triad talk and ask everyone to work individually. Write on the board: 'a = (3, 4), b = (1, −2). (1) Draw the tip-to-tail arrow diagram. (2) Write the resultant in component form. (3) Calculate the magnitude. Show all working.' Give students 4 minutes to draw and calculate, then collect the responses.
choices.tradeoff: Solo synthesis gives you the clearest, most honest picture of each student's ability to integrate across representations — uncontaminated by triad talk. Rajan's response will show full integration. But the move to individual work kills the emerging talk momentum. Siti, who was echoing her partners, will likely revert to her tip-to-tail error without the scaffold to correct her.

A1.outcome: You draw all three representations on the board simultaneously and connect them explicitly, including the tip-to-tail correction. The room goes quiet as students watch. Nur looks at the board and you see the moment of recognition — she can see that the components of the resultant are just the horizontal and vertical distances in the diagram, and that Pythagoras gives the length. She re-attempts the problem and writes: 'Resultant = (4, 2). Magnitude = √(4²+2²) = √20 = 2√5.' Wei Jie watches the board and writes the connection in his notes: 'Components = horizontal and vertical distances. Magnitude = Pythagoras on the components.' He re-attempts and gets the magnitude correct. Rajan copies the board notes compliantly but his body language shows disengagement — he already understood this. Siti looks at the tip-to-tail correction on the board and re-draws her diagram correctly. She copies the component calculation and magnitude from the board. When you circulate and ask 'Can you try a new problem?', she hesitates and does not write anything without the board example in front of her. On the next problem, 19 of 24 students now connect their representation to the magnitude calculation, up from 8. The majority gap has been addressed. But Siti is still dependent on copying, and Rajan is under-challenged.
A1.tradeoff: The whole-class reteach with simultaneous representations is efficient and reaches the majority who could not connect their preferred mode to the magnitude calculation. Process-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) addressed the specific gap directly and corrected Siti's tip-to-tail error. Nur and Wei Jie made clear gains. But the reteach treats the class as uniform — Rajan is under-challenged and Siti remains dependent on copying rather than building genuine understanding.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose to show all three representations simultaneously rather than building from one to the others? What were you prioritising?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Most students now connect their representation to the magnitude, but Rajan is disengaged and Siti is still copying. What does this tell you about whole-class reteaching in a diverse-profiles classroom?

How do you design a lesson so that students who prefer different representations are challenged to integrate across modes — not just work in their preferred one?

A2.outcome: You pair Rajan with Nur and ask Rajan to give AQS feedback on Nur's diagram. Rajan looks at Nur's diagram and begins: 'Your tip-to-tail construction is correct — the resultant is right. But you have not calculated the magnitude. How do you find the length of the resultant from the diagram?' Nur looks at the diagram and says: 'I could measure it?' Rajan responds: 'Or use the components — the resultant goes 4 right and 2 up, so the magnitude is √(4²+2²) = √20.' Nur writes the calculation. Wei Jie is paired with a student who has a complete component solution. His partner looks at Wei Jie's verbal description and says: 'You said northeast — can you draw the diagram?' Wei Jie draws the tip-to-tail diagram and his partner connects: 'The components are the horizontal and vertical distances — 4 right and 2 up. Magnitude = √20.' Wei Jie writes the calculation. Siti is paired with a student who notices her tip-to-tail error. Her partner says: 'Your second vector starts at the origin — it should start at the tip of the first vector.' Siti re-draws the diagram correctly. But when you ask her 'Why does the second vector start at the tip?', she says 'Because my partner said so.' The room is engaged and several students are making real gains. But Siti has not moved from copying to understanding, and two triads where all students preferred the same representation reinforced single-mode reasoning.
A2.tradeoff: AQS feedback activated the expertise in the room. Rajan deepened Nur's understanding of the diagram-to-magnitude connection, Wei Jie's partner helped him translate verbal reasoning into a diagram and magnitude, and Siti's partner caught the tip-to-tail error. But Siti cannot explain why the tip-to-tail rule works. Pairs where both students preferred the same representation reinforced single-mode reasoning.
A2.reflection:
Why did you choose peer feedback over whole-class reteaching? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Nur, Wei Jie, and Siti all made some gains, but Siti cannot explain the tip-to-tail rule and some pairs reinforced single-mode reasoning. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive peer feedback in a diverse-profiles classroom?

How do you design pair groupings so that students with different representation preferences are paired together — not just students with different levels of understanding?

B1.outcome: You pause the class and ask Rajan: 'From your diagram — why does the magnitude use Pythagoras?' Rajan responds: 'Because the components form a right triangle — the horizontal component is one leg, the vertical component is the other leg, and the resultant is the hypotenuse. Pythagoras gives the length of the hypotenuse.' You ask a second student to connect this to the component form: 'In component form, the resultant is (4, 2) — 4 is the horizontal distance and 2 is the vertical distance. The magnitude is the distance from the origin to the point (4, 2), which is √(4²+2²) by Pythagoras.' A third student connects to the tip-to-tail rule: 'And the tip-to-tail rule ensures the resultant goes from the start of the first vector to the end of the last — that is the total displacement.' The IRF chain produces a rich, class-built explanation linking all three representations to the magnitude and the tip-to-tail rule. Nur listens carefully and re-attempts the magnitude calculation. Wei Jie follows the chain and writes the Pythagoras connection. Siti watches but does not contribute. When you ask 'Does that make sense?', she nods quickly without speaking. Her corrected diagram is on her page, but she has not written any new reasoning. The chain has deepened understanding for the middle group and stretched Rajan, but Siti's conceptual gap remains unaddressed.
B1.tradeoff: The IRF chain leverages Rajan's diagram work and builds a rich, class-constructed explanation linking all three representations to the magnitude and the tip-to-tail rule. It stretched Rajan and deepened Nur's and Wei Jie's understanding. But the chain is driven by students who can articulate their reasoning — Siti's conceptual gap is not surfaced or addressed by the discussion.
B1.reflection:
Why did you choose to run an IRF chain using Rajan's diagram work? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? The chain produced strong cross-representation reasoning, but Siti's conceptual gap remains unaddressed. What does this tell you about the limits of whole-class discussion when some students are still building their first representation?

How do you design IRF probes that include students who are still working in a single mode rather than leaving them as passive observers?

B2.outcome: You write the new problem on the board — 'a = (2, 3), b = (4, −1). Find a + b and its magnitude using at least two representations' — and ask students to attempt it with a self-check. Rajan completes it quickly and writes a detailed self-check: 'I used components and the arrow diagram. Components: (2+4, 3+(−1)) = (6, 2). Magnitude: √(6²+2²) = √40 = 2√10. Diagram: tip-to-tail construction confirms the resultant points northeast with a small upward angle. I know this is correct because both representations give the same resultant.' His response shows deep integration and metacognitive awareness. Nur uses the diagram and components: she draws the tip-to-tail construction and reads off the components (6, 2), then calculates the magnitude. Her self-check says: 'I used the diagram to find the direction and the components to calculate the magnitude.' Wei Jie writes the components and a rough diagram but cannot explain why Pythagoras gives the magnitude: his self-check says 'I used components and a diagram. I used Pythagoras because that is the formula.' Siti draws the tip-to-tail diagram correctly — the scaffold has helped her internalise the rule — but does not write the components or calculate the magnitude. Her self-check is blank. The transfer task has revealed a clear split: Rajan and Nur can integrate across representations; Wei Jie has representations but not the conceptual connection; Siti can draw the diagram but cannot connect it to the calculation. 13 minutes remain.
B2.tradeoff: Scaffold removal tests integration honestly. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine cross-representation understanding. The self-check reflection (AfL Triangle) gives you individual evidence of reasoning. But Wei Jie has representations without the conceptual connection — he knows the formula but not why it works. Siti can now draw the diagram correctly but cannot connect it to the component calculation.
B2.reflection:
Why did you choose to test integration with a self-check application? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur succeeded, but Wei Jie has representations without the conceptual connection and Siti cannot connect the diagram to the calculation. What does this tell you about the difference between representation fluency and conceptual understanding in vector addition?

How do you design tasks that build the connection between the arrow diagram and the component calculation — not just the ability to draw a diagram or apply a formula?

C1.outcome: You write the shallow explanation on the board: 'The resultant vector points northeast because the x-component is positive and the y-component is positive.' You ask the class to give AQS feedback. Several students affirm the direction description. Nur raises her hand and questions: 'How do you calculate the exact magnitude? What is the length of the resultant?' A third student suggests: 'Use the components to calculate the magnitude: |a+b| = √(x²+y²) — this is Pythagoras applied to the component right triangle.' You write the complete AQS response on the board and ask all students to add the magnitude calculation to their explanation. Nur re-attempts and writes the magnitude calculation correctly. Wei Jie follows the AQS feedback and writes: 'Magnitude = √(4²+2²) = √20.' Rajan listens with mild interest — he was already correct. Siti copies the AQS response from the board line by line. When you ask her to attempt a new problem independently, she hesitates and does not write anything without the board example. The AQS process has made the representation-to-magnitude connection explicit for the whole class, but Siti's tip-to-tail error is not addressed and she is still copying rather than reasoning.
C1.tradeoff: The AQS structure made the missing representation-to-magnitude connection explicit and gave the single-mode triads a concrete model of integrated reasoning. Nur and Wei Jie made genuine gains. But Siti's tip-to-tail error is not addressed by the AQS feedback, and she is still copying from the board rather than reasoning independently. The AQS feedback served the middle group but missed the most fundamental error in the room.
C1.reflection:
Why did you choose to use AQS feedback on the common single-mode explanation rather than addressing the tip-to-tail error directly? What were you prioritising?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Nur and Wei Jie made gains, but Siti's tip-to-tail error persists and she is still copying. What does this tell you about the limits of whole-class feedback when a student has a fundamental diagram error?

How do you address a tip-to-tail error like Siti's in a diverse-profiles classroom — where students are working in different modes and errors are hidden behind different types of representations?

C2.outcome: The room goes quiet as students write their individual syntheses. Rajan writes a complete, integrated response: 'a = (3, 4), b = (1, −2). Components: (3+1, 4+(−2)) = (4, 2). Magnitude: √(4²+2²) = √20 = 2√5. Arrow diagram: tip-to-tail construction — b starts at the tip of a, resultant goes from origin to tip of b. The magnitude is the length of the resultant, which is the hypotenuse of the right triangle formed by the components.' His response shows full integration across all three representations. Nur writes the components and magnitude correctly and draws the tip-to-tail diagram: 'Resultant = (4, 2). Magnitude = √20. Diagram shows the resultant pointing northeast.' Her response is correct and complete — the triad talk has helped her make the connection. Wei Jie writes the components and magnitude correctly but draws the diagram with the second vector starting from the origin — the tip-to-tail error. His explanation says: 'Resultant = (4, 2). Magnitude = √20. The vectors add up.' Siti draws the diagram with the same tip-to-tail error as Wei Jie. She writes '(4, 2)' but does not calculate the magnitude. The individual writing has given you the clearest picture of each student's integration — from Rajan's complete synthesis to Siti's partial diagram. But the move to solo writing has revealed that the triad talk did not correct Wei Jie's tip-to-tail error or build Siti's calculation confidence.
C2.tradeoff: Individual written synthesis gives you the clearest, most honest evidence of each student's cross-representation understanding. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine integration. But the move to solo writing revealed that the triad talk did not correct Wei Jie's tip-to-tail error or build Siti's calculation confidence. The talk produced surface connections rather than genuine integration for most students.
C2.reflection:
Why did you move from triad talk to individual written synthesis? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed genuine integration, but Wei Jie has the tip-to-tail error and Siti cannot calculate the magnitude. What does this tell you about when collaborative talk builds genuine cross-representation understanding versus surface-level agreement?

How do you design a lesson that builds the connection between the arrow diagram and the component calculation for students who prefer different representations — without leaving any student with a fundamental diagram error uncorrected?
